SCENE IDENTITY
This is a low-density residential wildfire scene in Santa Rosa, California, featuring widely spaced single-family homes set among dense oak and mixed deciduous tree canopy on gently rolling terrain. Buildings are predominantly one- and two-story structures with composite asphalt shingle roofs appearing medium-gray, dark gray, or brown-red in the pre-fire imagery. Parcels are large with dry tan-brown grass clearings between tree clusters.

INTACT BUILDING ANCHOR
An intact building in this scene has a medium-gray or dark gray asphalt shingle roof (occasionally brown/terracotta) that appears solid, uniformly colored, and fully covering its footprint in the AFTER image, matching the BEFORE footprint exactly with no holes, charring, or structural gaps. Surrounding vegetation may be scorched but the roof surface itself is continuous and unbroken.

DAMAGE LEVEL DEFINITIONS

**no-damage:** Roof is fully intact, uniform color, no charring visible on structure. Footprint matches BEFORE exactly. (Note: this class is not present in this scene, but use as baseline.)

**minor-damage:** The building is clearly still standing with its roof largely intact — MORE THAN 50% of the roof surface is visibly present and covering the structure. Damage is limited to: surface scorching or discoloration on roof edges or one side, minor debris on roof, small burn patches affecting less than half the roof area, or slight darkening from smoke/heat. The building silhouette and wall structure are fully intact. From overhead, the roof reads as a mostly-complete shape with localized blemishes.

**major-damage:** The building is still structurally present and recognizable as a standing structure, BUT significant damage is evident: between roughly 50–90% of the roof is missing, collapsed inward, or heavily charred/burned through. You can see exposed interior or darkened open areas through the roof, partial wall collapse on one or more sides, or heavy charring across most of the footprint. Critically — WALLS OR FOUNDATION ARE STILL VISIBLE AND THE BUILDING SHAPE IS STILL RECOGNIZABLE. If you can see any standing walls, partial roof framing, or a recognizable building outline, it is NOT destroyed.

**destroyed:** The building is essentially gone. The footprint shows only rubble, ash, bare concrete foundation slab, or scattered debris. No roof remains. No walls are standing. The location appears as a flat, gray-white ash patch or bare slab where a building once stood. Compared to BEFORE, the building has effectively vanished and been replaced by debris or bare ground.

SPECIFIC FIX FOR THE CONFUSION

**Minor-damage over-escalated to major-damage (3 cases):** In this scene, minor-damage buildings retain most of their roof. Ask yourself: is MORE THAN HALF the roof surface still visibly present as a solid covering? If yes — even with visible scorching, edge damage, or partial discoloration — that is minor-damage, not major. A roof that is 60–70% intact with burn marks on one side is MINOR. Do not escalate to major just because charring is visible or because surrounding trees are burned. The threshold for major-damage requires that the roof is predominantly missing or collapsed inward, exposing the interior.

**Minor-damage over-escalated to destroyed (4 cases):** This is the most critical error. In this scene, burned tree canopy and scorched dry grass surrounding a building can make the entire area look like a destruction zone. But if the building's roof is still present — even partially darkened or scorched — it is NOT destroyed. Check: is there a recognizable roof shape still covering the footprint? If yes, it cannot be destroyed. Destroyed means the roof is completely gone and only a slab or ash remains.

**Major-damage predicted as destroyed (1 case):** If ANY walls are standing, ANY roof framing is visible, or the building footprint shows structural remnants above ground level, it is major-damage — NOT destroyed. Destroyed requires the complete absence of standing structure. A heavily burned shell with partial walls still qualifies as major-damage.

**Destroyed predicted as minor-damage (1 case):** If the AFTER image shows only a flat gray/white ash patch, bare concrete slab, or low rubble pile where a building stood in BEFORE — with no roof and no walls — that is destroyed. Do not mistake a clean ash slab for a lightly damaged building. Compare carefully to BEFORE: if the building volume has disappeared and only a flat remnant remains, classify as destroyed.

FALSE CUES
1. **Burned tree canopy surrounding a building** — scorched or missing trees around a structure do NOT indicate building destruction. Always evaluate the building footprint itself, not the surrounding vegetation.
2. **Shadow and dark patches on intact roofs** — in this heavily treed scene, shadows from surviving trees can create dark patches on roofs that mimic charring or holes. Verify that apparent dark areas are structural damage and not shadow artifacts.
3. **Dry tan grass and bare soil** — the pre-fire scene already has extensive dry brown grass and bare earth. Post-fire bare ground near a building is not evidence of destruction unless the building footprint itself is gone.

CLASSIFICATION GATES

DESTROYED GATE: label destroyed only if AT LEAST 3 are clearly visible:
  1. Footprint is rubble, ash, or bare foundation
  2. Roof completely gone
  3. Walls largely collapsed
  4. Building appears missing vs BEFORE
→ If gate PASSES: destroyed. If fails but significant damage: major-damage.

MAJOR-DAMAGE GATE: choose major-damage if AT LEAST 2 are clearly visible:
  1. More than half roof area missing or collapsed
  2. Partial wall collapse on one or more sides
  3. Large exposed interior visible
  4. Heavy charring across most of footprint
→ If gate PASSES: major-damage.

MINOR-DAMAGE: choose if ANY surface damage visible and gates not met.
NO DAMAGE: only if completely unchanged from BEFORE.

TIEBREAKER RULES
